coredata.dc:title: Preparation, characterization and in vitro anticancer performance of nanoconjugate based on carbon quantum dots and 5-Fluorouracil
coredata.dc:creator: Cutrim, Elaine S.M.
Vale, André A.M.
Manzani, Danilo
Barud, Hernane S.
Rodríguez-Castellón, Enrique
Santos, Ana P.S.A.
Alcântara, Ana C.S.
coredata.dc:description: This study is focused on the development of a nanodevice for loading and release of 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) with a view to improving its therapeutic efficiency, using as strategy the fabrication of a nanoconjugate through drug anchorage on the surface of carbon quantum dots (CQD). Several physicochemical and analytical techniques were employed to obtain information about materials morphology, structure, and optical properties. The results indicated that the interactions between both entities resulted in good physicochemical properties and photostability. Acid pH favored drug release, indicating a tendency to release 5-FU from 5-FU-CQD into the tumor microenvironment. The cytotoxicity of CQD and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate was evaluated against normal human lung fibroblast (GM07492A) and human breast cancer (MCF-7) cell lines. The CQD was non-toxic, indicating that these materials are biocompatible and can be used as a nanocarrier for 5-FU in biological systems. For the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, it was observed a reduction in toxicity for normal cells compared to free 5-FU, suggesting that drug anchoring in CQD reduced drug-associated toxicity, while for cancer cells exhibited an antitumor effect equivalent to that of the free drug, opening perspectives for the application of this material in anticancer therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of N-doped carbon quantum dots composition and structure, (B) chemical structure of 5-Fluorouracil, (C) CQD synthesis procedure and (B) 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate materials preparation using different initial concentrations of 5-FU.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of (A) CQD and (B) 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate. Emission spectra of (C) CQD and (D) 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 540 nm with increments of 20 nm. (E) 5-FU-CQD aqueous solution under daylight (left) and excited via a 365 nm UV light lamp (right) Inset in panel A shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of citric acid and urea. Inset in panel B exhibits the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of 5-FU solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM images of (A, B) CQD and (C, D) 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate.
Structural characterization of 5-FU, CQD and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate evaluated by (A) DRX, (B) Raman (1000–1800 cm−1), (C) FT-IR spectroscopy (4000–400 cm−1) and (D) XPS analysis.

TG and DTA curves of (A) pure CQD, (B) free 5-FU and (C) 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, and (D) DTG curves obtained for the CQD, 5-FU and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate. The analyses were carried out in dynamic air synthetic atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.
Photocatalytic stability of (A) free 5-FU and (B) 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate monitored by UV–Vis spectroscopy.

In vitro release profile of 5-FU from 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate in different pH values. (B) Cytotoxicity results of CQD to GM07492A cells estimated by MTT assay after incubated different concentrations for 24 and 48 h. Free 5-FU and 5-FU-CQD cytotoxicity to (C) GM07492A cells or (D) MCF-7 cells after incubated at different concentrations for 48 h. Statistically significant values were represented by *(p < 0.05), **(p < 0.005) and ***(p < 0.001). The inset in Fig. 7A showed the 5-FU release profile until 48 h of experiment.

5-FU-CQD nanoconjugates prepared by adsorption using different initial amounts of 5-FU from aqueous solutions. The amount of adsorbed 5-FU by CQD as well as the drug loading efficiency (DLE) percentage for each 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate is determined by UV–Vis spectrometer.

head.ce:title: Preparation, characterization and in vitro anticancer performance of nanoconjugate based on carbon quantum dots and 5-Fluorouracil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study is focused on the development of a nanodevice for loading and release of 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) with a view to improving its therapeutic efficiency, using as strategy the fabrication of a nanoconjugate through drug anchorage on the surface of carbon quantum dots (CQD). Several physicochemical and analytical techniques were employed to obtain information about materials morphology, structure, and optical properties. The results indicated that the interactions between both entities resulted in good physicochemical properties and photostability. Acid pH favored drug release, indicating a tendency to release 5-FU from 5-FU-CQD into the tumor microenvironment. The cytotoxicity of CQD and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate was evaluated against normal human lung fibroblast (GM07492A) and human breast cancer (MCF-7) cell lines. The CQD was non-toxic, indicating that these materials are biocompatible and can be used as a nanocarrier for 5-FU in biological systems. For the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, it was observed a reduction in toxicity for normal cells compared to free 5-FU, suggesting that drug anchoring in CQD reduced drug-associated toxicity, while for cancer cells exhibited an antitumor effect equivalent to that of the free drug, opening perspectives for the application of this material in anticancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) are the result of an association of genetic, physiological, environmental, and behavioral factors that currently account for 71% of all deaths globally. Cancer is the second leading cause of deaths from NCDs [1], where predictions suggest that by 2040, near to 16.4 million people will die of cancer, representing an increase of 71.1% compared to 2018 estimative [2]. Nowadays, chemotherapy is the most conventional approach for cancer treatment, nevertheless, despite notable progress made in this area, this therapy strategy shows many disadvantages like low therapeutic effectiveness, poor bioavailability and, high non-productive drug distribution due to the lack of specificity for tumors [3,4]. The non-selectively of anticancer drugs leads to serious consequences such as undesirable side effects, multiples drug resistance, and reduced drug concentration at the target location [5,6].

Recently, nanomedicine has made remarkable efforts to overcome the drawbacks associated with traditional cancer therapies, enabling substantial progress in basic research and clinical applications. The development of smart drug delivery systems (DDS) based on nanoparticles (also referred to as nanocarriers) has demonstrated to be capable to enhance chemotherapeutic drug efficiency due to their ability to improve pharmacokinetics, reduce toxicities, controlling the release and, delivering the active agent in a site-specific location [7–9]. These nanosystems can also passively target and accumulate in solid tumors due to enhanced permeation and retention effect, whereas drug concentration remained at safe levels in healthy tissues, enabling an increase in therapeutic efficacy and reducing the side effects, simultaneously [6]. Nano-DDS may also increase the stability and solubility of anticancer drugs which is of great importance since more than 40% of these drugs have poor water dispersibility. This is one of the major limitations associated with intravenous administration of chemotherapeutic agents, thus the association with nanomaterials can increase the bioavailability and biodistribution of drugs [10]. As a result of the great performance of the nano-DDS for cancer therapy, the Food and Drug Administration had approved several nanosystems for different types of cancer at different stages such as Doxil® [11], Abraxane® [12] and, Onivyde® [13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQD) (Fig. 1A) are a new class of carbon nanomaterial that recently emerged and have gained considerable attention from researchers due to their great potential for a wide range of technological applications. Synthesized for the first time in 2004 [14], CQD are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm that can be modified according to the synthetic route and/or precursor selected [15,16]. They display extraordinary properties, including high photostability, tunable fluorescence spectra, chemical inertness, easy surface functionalization, aqueous solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [17,18], becoming ideal candidates for biomedical applications, including as inhibitors of host cells infection by HCoV-229E coronavirus [19], biosensors [20] or drug delivery systems for antineoplastic agents [21–23]. In this context, it was recently reported that carbon dots passivated with polyethylene glycol were loaded with methotrexate to obtain a nanosystem that showed effective tumor growth inhibition against MCF-7 cells and also had great potential for bioimaging applications [24]. Moreover, organophilic CQD employed as nanocarriers for the anticancer drug curcumin exhibited high loading efficiency and, more importantly, facilitated the internalization of curcumin into cells, which significantly accelerated the apoptosis of HeLa cells [25]. In addition, an innovative hyaluronic acid-modified carbon dot-doxorubicin nanoparticle demonstrated that the active target enhanced in vivo tumor accumulation and, consequently led to superior anti-tumor efficacy of the nanosystem compared to free DOX [26]. Based on these studies, it is possible to assume that associations of CQD with anticancer drugs could result in DDS with enhanced therapeutic effects in cancer treatment [27].

5-Fluorouracil (5-FU, Fig. 1B) is an antineoplastic agent widely used for the treatment of colorectal, breast, gastrointestinal, head, and neck cancers [28,29]. Since its first synthesis in 1957 [30], 5-FU had been used in chemotherapy and currently is quoted in the Model List of Essential Medicines reported by WHO which corroborates this is a well-established drug in cancer therapy [31]. Nevertheless, 5-FU efficiency is limited by its short half-life in blood plasma – varying between 10 and 15 min – and non-specificity, demanding frequent dosing that often induces systematic toxicity [32,33]. The high levels of drug delivered to normal tissues result in several side effects, such as myelotoxicity, hand-foot syndrome, gastrointestinal mucositis, neuro, and cardiotoxicities which may lead to a decrease of drug dose or discontinuation of treatment [33–35]. In order to improve the efficacy of cancer therapy and ensure patient safety, the association of the 5-FU to a delivery system can be seen as an interesting approach.

In this work, it was assumed that CQD have the ability to associate with 5-FU, resulting in a nanoconjugate system with enhanced physicochemical and release properties compared to free 5-FU, reducing the cytotoxicity effect, which could help make the treatment more effective and safer for patients compared to conventional therapies. It was reported in the literature studies with systems that possessed CQD and 5-FU [36,37], nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to report the direct association between 5-FU and CQD in a nanoconjugate system with a very extensive physicochemical characterization and evaluation of the effect in biological system.

5-Fluorouracil (purity ≥99%) and dialysis membrane (Molecular weight cut off = 2000 Da) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. For CQD synthesis, citric acid (CA, 99.5%) and urea (99%) were obtained from Dinâmica, dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%) was provided by Exôdo Científica, and NaOH pellets were supplied by Carlo Erba. Buffer solutions simulating different physiological environments were prepared from salts of analytical grade: Na2HPO4 (>99%, Dinâmica), KH2PO4 (>99%, Dinâmica), NaCl (>99%, Isofar), acetic acid, and sodium acetate (>99%, Isofar). Double distilled water was used in all the preparations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots and 5-Fluorouracil-based nanoconjugate (5-FU-CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped CQD were obtained following a procedure reported by Qu and coworkers [38] that was described in supplementary information and also represented in Fig. 1C. The conjugation of 5-Fluorouracil on CQD was carried out by adding 10 mg of CQD in different concentrations of an aqueous solution of 5-FU as represented in Fig. 1D. In this procedure, the drug concentration used varied in the range of 100 to 800 mg L−1, resulting in nanoconjugate materials with different 5-FU: CQD ratios. The resultant solution was allowed to react for 24 h under vigorous stirring at 23 °C. After that, the solution was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 2000 Da) against bidistilled water for 2 h to remove any unreacted 5-FU. Finally, the 5-FU-CQD solution located inside the dialysis membrane was freeze-dried for 72 h to obtain a dried solid product. The synthesized CQD and the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate were properly characterized by different physicochemical techniques as described in the supplementary information.

To evaluate drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug loading content (DLC) of 5-FU in nanoconjugate materials, the amount of free drug outside of the dialysis membrane was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-1800) at a wavelength of 266 nm and calculated using a calibration curve applying the Lambert-Beer law. DLE and DLC were assessed using the following equations [39]:
(1)

where A5-FU and AFREE denoted the initial amount of 5-FU (mg), the unreacted amount of 5-FU (mg) after loading reaction, and mCQD refers to the mass of the nanocarrier (mg) used. All measurements were performed in triplicate and the values were represented as mean ± SD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photostability of the 5-FU and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate

ce:list-item.ce:para: To evaluate the photostability of the free drug and the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, an aqueous solution prepared using water for injection (Isofarma) containing 1.5 mg of 5-FU or the necessary amount of the nanoconjugate that contains 1.5 mg of 5-FU (final pH values between 6.0 and 7.0) was transferred to a glass reactor (100 mL), and a mercury vapor lamp without bulb (125 W) operating at 354 nm was used as UV light source being placed 10 cm above the reactor. The temperature inside the reactor was kept at 20 ± 0.5 °C by means of a thermo-controlled water bath, where water circulated through a reactor jacket in order to ensure the temperature constant in the system. At predetermined time intervals of irradiation, aliquots of 3 mL were withdrawn, and the band associated with 5-FU at 266 nm was monitored by UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-1800).

ce:section.ce:para:
The in vitro release profiles of 5-FU from 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate were obtained using the dialysis technique [28] at pH 7.4 (PBS) and 5.0 (acetate buffer). These pH values were chosen to simulate different environments of the human body, specifically the physiological environment and the interstitial fluid of cancer, respectively [40]. Briefly, 5 mg of freeze-dried 5-FU-CQD were dispersed in 10 mL of buffer solution and placed into a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 2000 Da) (Fig. S1). Subsequently, this system was immersed in the respective release medium and kept at 37 °C under mild stirring. At specific time intervals, aliquots of the buffer solution outside the dialysis membrane were collected and the amount of 5-FU released was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-1800) at a wavelength of 266 nm as described previously. The percentage of 5-FU released from the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate was calculated according to the following equation:
(3)
Cumulative release
%
=
Amount of
5 − FU
released
Total amount of
5 − FU
loadedin 5 − FU − CQD
× 100

Bank of Cells of Rio de Janeiro – NCE/UFRJ (http://bcrj.org.br/). Cell maintenance and proliferation procedures were performed following manufacturer recommendations. Briefly, the MCF-7 cell line (human breast cancer) were cultured in a CO2 (5%) stove at 37 °C in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and supplemented with 10% v/v fetal bovine serum (FBS), 50 U mL−1 penicillin/50 μg ml−1 streptomycin and 2 mM l-glutamine (Invitrogen). As a control, the cell line of normal human lung fibroblasts (GM07492A), which was also cultured in DMEM medium supplemented according to the Coriell Institute for Medical Research (https://www.coriell.org).

The viability of GM07492A and MCF-7 cells treated with CQD, free 5-FU, and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate (equivalent concentration of 5-FU) was evaluated by a standard MTT assay. At first, cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1.0 × 104 cells in 100 μL of DMEM medium per well and incubated for 24 h with a suitable growth medium to allow cell attachment. Thereafter, the culture medium was removed and fresh medium containing CQD, free 5-FU, or 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate were added to each well, and then the plate was reincubated at 37 °C for 24 or 48 h. The final concentration of CQD ranged from 12 to 800 mg L−1, which includes the necessary concentration of CQD for nanoconjugate fabrication, whereas the drug concentration was tested in the range of 0.25 to 16.0 mg L−1 for both free 5-FU and 5-FU-CQD groups. After each incubation time, the content in wells was removed and the cells were washed with PBS to remove any residual particle. For MTT reaction, 100 μL of fresh medium containing 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added in which well and incubated for 3 h for formazan crystals formation. Afterward, the medium was aspirated and a 200 μL solution of 10% SDS and HCl (0.01 N) was added to dissolve the above-mentioned crystals. The absorbance of the purple formazan crystals was measured at 570 nm using (EPOCH, Bio Tek) microplate reader [41]. Cell viability was estimated by the following equation:
(4)
Cell viability
%
=
Absorbance for treated cells Absorbance for control cells
× 100
where the absorbance for untreated cells was taken as 100% cell viability.

All results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) of independent experiments. Data were analyzed via two-way ANOVA using Graph-Pad Prism 5.0. Statistically significant values were represented by *(p < 0.05), **(p < 0.005) and ***(p < 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain N-doped CQD using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen doping agent, a solvothermal treatment approach was employed. Citric acid is a favorable precursor for CQD synthesis due to the active functional groups (-COOH and –OH) present in the structure, besides its low cost and easy availability. In addition, nitrogen doping can generate different surface states enhancing the photostability of CQD [42]. After solvothermal treatment, the initially colorless reaction mixture turns a deep brown color, suggesting the formation of CQD. As shown in Fig. S2, the aqueous solution of purified CQD displays a purple color. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the starting materials, i.e., citric acid and urea (see the inset of Fig. 2A) not revealed any obvious absorption over the analyzed range. Nonetheless, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared CQD (Fig. 2A) exhibits a shoulder around 276 nm and bands at 340 and 535 nm, implying the building of a new structure. The shoulder at 276 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bond in the aromatic domains of the CQD core, whereas the bands at 340 and 535 nm are both attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bonds from different surface oxygen-containing functional groups on the shell of CQD [43,44]. It also can be noted a shoulder near to the visible region, suggesting that the as-prepared CQD have different surface states. The functional groups on the CQD surface are one of the most important features that guarantee their application in a wide range of fields, including in the biomedical area. Regarding that, the surface of CQD are used as nanoplatforms for the assembly of 5-FU, an active molecule with anticancer properties against a variety of solid tumors. Fig. 2B shows the UV–Vis spectrum of 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate prepared using an initial 5-FU solution at 400 mg L−1. The band observed in the inset of Fig. 2B at 266 nm is characteristic of free 5-FU, and its presence in the nanoconjugate absorption spectrum may suggest the successful loading of the drug by the CQD. In addition, the band at 535 nm, related to the CQD surface state, is shifted towards higher wavelengths, indicating possible interactions between 5-FU and CQD.

In an effort to investigate the affinity of 5-FU molecules on the CDQs surface, different initials concentrations of the drug were used, resulting in nanoconjugates materials with distinct 5-FU contents. From the adsorbed amounts of 5-FU in different 5-FU-CQD samples, an adsorption isotherm is obtained (Fig. S3). According to Giles [45], the obtained curve can be classified as L-type (Langmuir) and the respective fit to its model is performed using the following equation:
(5)
Q e
=
Q m
.
K L
.
C e
1 +
K L
.
C e

where Qe represents the adsorbed amount of 5-FU per mass of CQD, Ce is the 5-FU equilibrium concentration, Qm is the maximum amount of adsorbed 5-FU and KL corresponds to the constant affinity between the 5-FU and CQD. The fitting to the Langmuir model is performed using 62.5% of the curve (R2 = 0.95176) and indicates a high affinity (KL = 0.07854 L.mg−1) between the adsorbate (5-FU) and the adsorbent (CQD). The constant KL can be related to the Gibbs energy of the adsorption process using the following equation [46]:
(6)
ΔG ads
= − RTlnK

where R was the constant, T is adsorption temperature and K corresponds to (KLρ/4)2 for ionic species [47], where ρ is the ratio of the solvent density to its molecular weight (ρ ≈ 1000/18). Therefore, the calculated Gibbs energy is ΔG = −58.42 kJ.mol−1, indicating a high tendency of 5-FU to be spontaneously adsorbed on the CQD surface. From the adsorption isotherm, it is also possible to infer that 5-FU molecules are adsorbed as a monolayer, providing progressive saturation of CQD active sites, which is typical behavior for Langmuir-type isotherms [45].

However, from Table 1 , which shows the initial amounts of drug used in the synthesis of 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate as well as the respective amount of 5-FU adsorbed on CDQ, it can be noted that for equilibrium concentrations higher than 8.8 mg L−1, the amounts of 5-FU adsorbed are greater than those corresponding to the plateau in the adsorption isotherm (Fig. S3), reaching values of around 1000 mg of 5-FU per gram of CQD. This behavior could suggest that adsorbed 5-FU originates a surface with nearly the same affinity for more drug molecules than the original surface of CQD [45], or may indicate the formation of molecular dimers in solution at higher concentrations of 5-FU, as previously reported in studies of molecular dynamics in water for this drug [48]. Besides the isotherm adsorption, the drug loading efficiency from different 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugates is also determined. From the results summarized in Table 1, it is observed that 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugates exhibit DLE higher than 50%. It is noteworthy that, to the best of our knowledge, no study on the direct self-assembly of 5-FU molecules in CQD is reported, which makes these results quite relevant. To conduct further experiments in this study, it is necessary to determine the most appropriate nanoconjugate, and the DLE percentage can be considered a good parameter for this purpose. Since the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate prepared with an initial drug concentration of 600 mg L−1 exhibit the highest DLE, and as indicated in Table 1, above this concentration a probable second 5-FU layer on CDQs takes place through drug-drug affinity, the 5-FU-CQD sample prepared with an initial concentration of 400 mg L−1 is chosen to ensure the direct assembly of 5-FU on the CQD. Henceforth, all the analyses and experiments are conducted with this nanoconjugate.

To investigate the optical properties of the CDQ and 5-FU-CDQ samples, photoluminescence analyses were conducted. As displayed in Fig. 2C, the emission spectra of CQD exhibit an excitation-independent behavior with a maximum intensity centered at 620 nm when excited at 500 nm. This optical property was reported to be originated from CQD with narrowed size distribution and similar surface states, in contrast to carbon dots with excitation-dependent PL property that can emerge from carbon dots mixture or nanoclusters aggregates [49–51]. Similar behavior is observed for the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate with a maximum emission intensity at 613 nm when excited at 520 nm (Fig. 2D), indicating that the CQD photoluminescent properties are preserved in the nanoconjugate. Moreover, when 5-FU-CQD is excited at wavelengths above 380 nm it is evidenced a second emission band at 540 nm, assigned to a dual-emissive behavior with the maintenance of the excitation-independent PL feature. This phenomenon indicates that the interactions between 5-FU and CQD functional groups generate a different surface state when compared to pure CQD, as also suggested by UV–Vis measurements (Fig. 2B). Upon UV excitation at 365 nm, the 5-FU-CQD aqueous solution emits in the red region (Fig. 2E), which is particularly interesting for biomedical applications (e.g. in vitro and in vivo bioimaging) because red light exhibits a deeper tissue penetration and reduced autofluorescence [43,52].

The morphology and size distribution of CQD were assessed by TEM (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3A and B, CQD exhibit a homogeneous distribution characterized by a spherical morphology with an average size of 4.82 ± 0.75 nm, with diameters ranging from 3.40 to 6.53 nm (Fig. S4), corroborating their small nature of less than 10 nm. On the other hand, the presence of 5-FU on CQD seems to favors the aggregation of the particles, where the nanoconjugate has not been separated from each other (Fig. 3C and D). Thus, the morphology presented by the nanoconjugate is relatively larger than pure CQD, displaying a size of approximately 15 nm. Similar behavior was reported by other authors in studies about the conjugation of doxorubicin (DOX) and CQD, where the average size of DOX-CQD particle is near to 20 nm [40].

The textural properties of CQD and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate were also investigated using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and, the obtained results were summarized in Table S1. CQD exhibits a surface area of 12.8 m2 g−1 (VT = 0,16 cm3/g), which is in agreement with that reported by Wang and coauthors that obtained SBET values of approximately 16.0 m2 g−1 for hollow carbon dots [53]. Meanwhile, 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate shows values of SBET and total pore volume (VT) of 26.5 m2 g−1 and 0.21 cm3/g, respectively, indicating that the immobilization of 5-FU on CQD promoted an increase of the surface area value by almost twice when compared to pure carbon nanoparticles. These textural properties can be beneficial in terms of biological applications since it was reported that the increase in surface area and porosity provides an improvement of the interaction between carbon-based material surface and cells [54].

Structural properties of CQD and the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate were investigated using XRD and Raman spectroscopy analysis as shown in Fig. 4 . The XRD of the CQD exhibits two reflection planes around 27.0° and 42.4° corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of the graphitic framework, respectively (Fig. 4A). Compared to ordered graphite structure (2θ = 26.5°; d002 = 0.34 nm), the shift to higher angles can be ascribed to disordered carbon (sp3) created during the carbonization process. In addition, the first reflection of CQD around 4.9° (d001 = 1.8 nm) is similar to reported to be the main peak of graphite oxide (2θ = 10.6°; d001 = 0.84 nm), showing in this case a displacement to lower 2θ angles as a consequence of the increase in interlayer spacing [55,56]. In this case, it is expected that during the synthesis process several functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylate, and amino groups bonded to the basal planes of the structure, inducing an increase in interlayer spacing. Regarding 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate (Fig. 4A), the intense peak evidenced at 2θ = 28.3° is correspondent to the (220) plane of pure 5-FU (2θ = 28.5°; d220 = 0.31 nm) [57] and indicates the successful assembly of 5-FU on CQD surface. This result is also confirmed by XRD patterns obtained from the mechanical mixture of 5-FU and CQD (Fig. S5) employing the same amount of drug and carbon nanoparticles found for the 5-FU-CQD selected sample, which evidenced a different diffractogram profile compared to 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, pointing out the synthesis of a new structure. In the Raman spectrum of the CQD (Fig. 4B) is identified four bands at 111.2, 1252.4, 1350.9, and 1551.0 cm− 1 and a shoulder around 1634.8 cm−1 which are ascribed to D″, D4, D, G, and D′ band, respectively. The G band, which shows an E2g symmetry, is assigned to the stretching vibration of sp2 carbon networks (Fig. S6A). Conversely, D band is a breathing mode of A1g (Fig. S6 B) symmetry, which is a feature induced by any kind of disorders or defects [58–60]. Therefore, it can be assumed that CQD are formed by a graphite-like structure with defect-rich regions, corroborating the XRD results. Although the 5-FU-CQD spectrum is very similar to that of pure CQD, the decrease in the intensity ratio of the D and the G bands (ID/IG) in the nanoconjugate which changed from 0.81 in the pure CQD to 0.53 is evidenced. Considering that the ID/IG intensity ratio is inversely proportional to the sp2 carbon amount, the decrease of ID/IG indicates a higher content of ordered carbons in the 5-FU-CQD sample. Thereby, the loading of 5-FU on the CQD surface increased the content of sp2 carbons likely due to the high crystallinity nature of this drug. Furthermore, minor blue shifts of the D″, D, G, and D′ bands were identified in the spectrum of 5-FU-CQD compared to pure CQD (Fig. 4B), which suggests probable drug-nanocarrier interactions in nanoconjugate.

To enlighten these possible interactions, FT-IR analyses were conducted for CQD, 5-FU and, 5-FU-CQD (Fig. 4C). For pure CQD, the absorption bands at 3548, 3474, and 3415 cm−1 are attributed to -OH stretching vibrations, implying a surface with abundant hydroxyl groups. Other characteristics bands of oxygen-containing functional groups include CO stretching (1676 cm−1), carboxylate antisymmetric and symmetric stretching (1616 and 1395 cm−1), and CO (1026 cm−1) stretching vibrations are observed. The bands at 1637 (C=C) and 619 cm−1 (ArH) indicate the formation of an aromatic structure in CQD core, while the CH stretching vibration at 2812 cm−1 corresponds to amorphous carbon in CQD, endorsing the XRD and Raman discussion. Moreover, the bands at 3234 and 1352 cm−1 which are ascribed to –NH2 and CN stretching vibrations, respectively, suggest the success N-doping of CQD during the synthesis process [60,61]. Concerning to 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, it is observed additional features related to NH, CO, and CF bonds from 5-FU molecule, indicating the successful loading of 5-FU on CQD [62,63]. In addition, small wavenumbers shift in the nanoconjugate involving characteristic bands from CQD (-OH, -NH and CO) and 5-FU (-NH, CF and CO) are observed, indicating possible drug-nanocarrier interactions. It is worthy to mention that the band assigned to the carbonyl group is displaced in the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate appearing at 1680 cm−1, which diverges with the correspondent wavenumber from pure CQD and free 5-FU, implying that interactions involving this group made a substantial contribution in the formation of 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate. These results could suggest that electrostatic interactions and/or hydrogen bonds are the main forces involved in the stabilization of 5-FU on the CQD nanoparticles. XPS analyses were also performed to determine elemental composition and obtain further insights about the chemical surface state of the samples (Fig. 4D). The CQD survey spectrum show three peaks at 284.7, 399.8, and 530.8 eV attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with the following atomic concentrations 58.89%, 10.99%, and 23.61%, respectively (Table S2), in addition, a discrete content of Na (6.51%) is observed in account to sodium hydroxide used during the purification process of CQD. The C 1s high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. S7) was deconvoluted into four contributions with binding energies values at 284.8, 286.6, 287.9, and 289.2 eV which are assigned to C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O, CO and COOH bonds, respectively, with the highest contribution assigned to CC (sp 2 and sp 3) interactions due to graphitic structure and adventitious carbon (Table S3) [64,65]. The N 1s core level spectrum (Fig. S8) shows a single peak at 399.5 eV typical of amine groups, which corroborates the interpretation of the CQD FT-IR spectrum [66]. The O 1s core level (Fig. S9) spectrum demonstrate three components with binding energies at 530.9, 532.6, and 535.0 eV attributed to CO (carbonyl and carboxylate), CO (oxygen single bonded) and C-OH bond, and Na KLL signal, respectively, because the presence of sodium was also detected, implying that the surface of CQD are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups [67]. For free 5-FU, the C 1s core level spectrum was decomposed into three contributions at 284.8 eV (29%), 286.6 eV (37%), and 288.8 eV (34%), which are assigned to adventitious carbon and -C=C- bond, CO, and amide, and CF, respectively. The N 1s, O 1s, and F 1s core level spectra for 5-FU show a single peak at 400.4, 531.6, and 687.4 eV, respectively; and are assigned to amide and CF bonds (Fig. S10) [68]. The found N/O and N/F atomic ratios are 0.99 and 2.45 near to the theoretical values (1.00 and 2.00). Concerning 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate, the XPS survey spectrum is similar to that displayed for pure CQD, nevertheless, the arising of the F 1s peak is observed, giving further evidence of nanoconjugate fabrication. The N 1s core level spectrum of the nanoconjugate can be decomposed into two contributions at 399.7 eV (88%) and 401.0 eV (12%). The second contribution comes from the presence of 5-FU and appears at a slighter shifted at high binding energy probably due to the interaction between the drug and the CQD. The F 1s core level spectrum for the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate shows a single peak at 687.6 eV, similar binding energy to that observed for the FU drug (687.4 eV). These values are typical of fluorine forming a covalent bond with carbon [68]. Essentially, FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 4C) and XPS (Fig. 4D) analyses demonstrate that the CQD are composed of an aromatic core and a surface co-functionalized with nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups that play a key role in 5-FU-CQD fabrication.

As a result of these abundant oxygen-containing groups (COO-, CO, -OH) in the CQD surface, a negative zeta potential of ξ = −32.7 ± 5.7 mV (Fig. S11) is observed. This negative charge was reported to guarantee great water dispersibility of CQD and long-term stability in aqueous solution [69]. 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate exhibits a zeta potential of ξ = −28.8 ± 2.9 mV, indicating an increase in 5-FU-CQD surface charge after drug loading. Since 5-FU shows an anionic nature, the increase observed in zeta potential values could be related to interactions between functional groups with an opposite charge from both CQD and 5-FU that ends up neutralizing the negative charge. To confirm this assumption, zeta potential measurement of a 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate synthesized with a higher initial drug concentration (500 mg L−1) was carried out. The zeta potential of this nanoconjugate turned out to be ξ = −24.1 ± 1.5 mV, corroborating the above hypothesis.

With the purpose to evaluate the thermal stability of CQD, 5-FU and 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate TG/DTG and DTA analysis were performed under synthetic air atmosphere, as shown in Fig. 5 . From room temperature to 143.6 °C, the pure CQD nanoparticles (Fig. 5A) demonstrate a mass loss of 11.2% corresponding to the endothermic event at 62.6 °C in the DTA curve, which is ascribed to the elimination of physically adsorbed water. The exothermic events at 445.0 °C and 513.3 °C observed in the DTA curve corresponding to a mass loss of 55.4% and could be associated with the CQD degradation process. These events are assigned to the removal of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylate, and amine from the CQD surface. The mass loss between 700 and 1000 °C can be attributed to the decomposition of aromatic domains in CQD core [70,71] that were reflected in the DTA curve as an endothermic event around 881.4 °C, resulting in a mass residue of 8.1%. For free 5-FU (Fig. 5B), a mass loss of 3.1% is observed up to 227.5 °C due to the removal of physically adsorbed water molecules. Above this temperature, the drug loses 83.7% of its mass in a rapid step, corresponding to the endothermic events at 280.5 °C and 318.2 °C that are associated with 5-FU melting and degradation process, respectively [72]. The 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate (Fig. 5C) demonstrates a thermal behavior quite similar to pure CQD. Although the melting event of free 5-FU is anticipated to lower temperatures in nanoconjugate, it is important to highlight that the step related to drug molecule degradation at 314.5 °C (Fig. 5B) is significantly displaced to higher temperatures (357 °C) in 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate as evidenced in the DTA (Fig. 5C) and DTG (Fig. 5D) curves, indicating an improvement of around 40 °C in thermal stability of the antineoplastic molecule when associated with the CQD. Similarly, the intense event assigned to the decomposition of CQD functional groups that occurs at 445.0 °C in pure CQD (Fig. 5A) is shifted to 474.0 °C in 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate (Fig. 5C) which corroborates the enhance in thermal stability of both 5-FU and CQD in the 5-FU-CQD material. All these improvements can be the result of good compatibility and the establishment of interactions between both moieties, as previously discussed.

Chemotherapeutic drugs such as 5-FU can undergo several physical-chemical processes, such as hydrolysis, oxidation, biodegradation, and photolysis. Studies based on stress degradation provide valuable information that can be used to develop manufacturing processes or to select proper packaging. The drug may be exposed to different stress conditions during storage and transport, leading to chemical instability of the drug that can be prevented by a suitable pharmaceutical form [73]. Therefore, the photocatalytic degradation profile of the free drug and 5-FU-CQD was assessed by UV–Vis spectrometry to evaluate the ability of the nanoconjugate to reduce 5-FU degradation. As shown in Fig. 6 , after 120 min of experiment, the solution of free 5-FU is almost completely degraded. In contrast, UV–Vis spectra of the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate seems do not show a complete degradation of 5-FU after 120 min of assay, demonstrating that the association with CQD could result in a decrease of the drug degradation rate, likely due to the ability of CQD to absorb radiation [74,75], minimizing the effects of degradation on the drug. However, it is observed in both spectra absorption shifts, which seems to be a characteristic of the 5-FU under photolytic stress [76], that could be associated with some possible degradation product of the 5-FU (loss of the fluorine atom (defluorination) of the drug molecule, for instance) [77,78]. Although other stability studies are necessary, this assay clearly demonstrates the significant improvement in photostability of the 5-FU in the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate compared to free 5-FU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release profile, cell viability and anticancer activity of 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate
ce:section.ce:para: An important feature of drug delivery systems is the capability of the nanocarrier agent to release the active molecule in a sustainable manner. With this purpose in mind, in vitro release assays of 5-FU-CQD were performed at pHs 7.4 and 5.0, simulating the physiological environment and tumor microenvironment, respectively. From the 5-FU cumulative release profile shown in Fig. 7A, it is possible to identify an initial burst release of antineoplastic agent followed by a sustained profile in both environments up to 48 h (inset in Fig. 7A). Nonetheless, in this in vitro assay, it is observed that pH 5.0 promoted a higher release of 5-FU up to 12 h of experiment, indicating that the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate has the ability to release higher amounts of the drug in fluids that simulate the microenvironment of tumor cells compared to the physiological environment. This behavior of the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate can be considerable advantageous in terms of cancer therapy efficiency, since the nanosystem is able to release 5-FU in the biological site for a certain time.

In this study, the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate is evaluated as a drug delivery system that improves security and efficacy in cancer treatment. For such biomedical purposes, the nanomaterial biocompatibility is a fundamental aspect. Thus, the cytotoxicity of CQD against normal human lung fibroblast line (GM07492A) was assessed using a standard MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 7B, CQD exhibit no cytotoxicity to GM07492A cells even at high doses of 800 μg mL−1. After incubation for 48 h, the cell viability is higher than 90% at all doses tested, suggesting that the as-prepared CQD are in fact ideal drug delivery nanocarriers. Interestingly, at some concentrations the survival rate of GM07492A cells exposed to CQD are significantly higher than the control group, indicating this material is not only biocompatible but also could promote cell proliferation. Similar results using carbon dots are reported in the literature [40,79], being cell growth stimulus attributed to CQD capability of acting as scaffolds mimicking the in vivo environment [80]. Similarly, the viability of normal cells treated with free 5-FU and 5-FU-CQD loaded with equivalent drug amounts were evaluated. As observed in Fig. 7C, a decline in cell viability is noticed in both groups with increasing in 5-FU concentration, confirming a dose-dependent behavior. Nevertheless, the cytotoxicity of 5-FU-CQD is considerably lower as concentration reached 2.00 μg mL−1 in comparison with free drug. For instance, at 8.00 and 16.00 μg mL−1 the difference in cell viability of 5-FU-CQD and the free 5-FU group is 36.94% and 43.87%, respectively. After 48 h, the viability of GM07492A cells treated with 5-FU-CQD is 82.13%, whereas in the 5-FU group is only 33.70%. These results strongly suggest that 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate has the ability to reduce the cytotoxicity associated with free drug to normal cells, which may be attributed to a slower release of 5-FU from the nanoconjugate [27,81]. Under equally treatment conditions, the anticancer activity of 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate and free 5-FU was evaluated using a human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7). It can be noted that in both groups the decrease in survival rate is observed only at doses above 1.00 μg mL−1 demonstrating a dose-dependent behavior (Fig. 7C). At higher concentrations, 5-FU-CQD exhibits a superior anticancer activity to MCF-7 cells in comparison with the free drug, demonstrating that 5-FU anchorage on the CQD surface do not interfere in the biological activity of the drug. The difference in viability for cells treated with 5-FU-CQD and the free drug is around 12%, nonetheless as shown in Fig. 7D these values do not show a statistically significant difference. Accordingly, it could be inferred that 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate exhibits an antitumor effect to MCF-7 cells analogous to free 5-FU, however with reduced toxicity to normal human cells. This suggests the great potential of 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate in cancer therapy since reducing the damage to normal cells decrease the side effects to patients and may increase tolerance to drug while inhibiting or eliminate tumor cells.

This study demonstrated the nanofabrication of a novel drug delivery system based on the self-assembly of the anticancer 5-FU onto carbon quantum dots surface. The CQD presented a heterogeneous crystalline structure with a substantial contribution of functional groups, such as hydroxyls, carbonyls, carboxylate, and amino groups which allowed the attachment of 5-FU molecules through electrostatic and/or hydrogen bonds, as pointed out by FTIR and Raman spectroscopy. From the differences observed between the CQD and 5-FU-CQD profile in several characterizations techniques employed, it was possible to confirm the successful formation of the nanoconjugate system. The acid pH promoted a higher release of the drug in the first 12 h, indicating that the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate had the ability to selectively release 5-FU in the tumor microenvironment when compared to the physiological environment. According to the MTT assay, CQD was non-cytotoxic to GM07492A cells even at the highest concentration tested, demonstrating that these nanoparticles were in fact biocompatible and therefore suitable for biomedical applications. Surprisingly, the CQD also promoted cell proliferation due to their capability of acting as scaffolds mimicking the in vivo environment, which could be promising for future studies. The 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate exhibited less toxicity to normal cells in comparison with free 5-FU at equivalent concentration, whereas against a breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) the nanoconjugate and the free 5-FU showed an equivalent anticancer activity. These results strongly suggest that the 5-FU-CQD nanoconjugate may increase the efficiency in cancer therapy by reducing the toxicity to normal cells related to free 5-FU, allowing the reduction of side effects that are quite uncomfortable for patients, but maintaining the therapeutic efficiency associated with 5-FU. These studies are extremely important for future in vivo tests, opening perspectives in the application of this material in cancer therapy.
